proofs.proof_tex: Let nu_star in N(pbar) have support cardinality K <= 3, and write nu_star = sum_{ell=1}^K w_ell delta_{u_ell} with w_ell > 0 and u_ell in [0,1]. Let N = M m and assume N pbar is an integer. Apply lem:finite-atomic-grid-split-rounding with nu = nu_star. It gives a vector pi_rd in Pi_{M,m}(pbar), produced by the grid-split rounding construction, and a coupling gamma_rd of nu_star with L_M(pi_rd) whose average displacement is at most 6/M + 2/m. Apply lem:quartic-variance-coupling-lipschitz to this coupling. With L_V = 2 |V_1| + 3 |V_3| + 8 |V_4|, we obtain |V(L_M(pi_rd)) - V(nu_star)| <= L_V (6/M + 2/m). Hence V(L_M(pi_rd)) <= V(nu_star) + 6 L_V (M^{-1} + m^{-1}). Taking the rounding constant C_rd(nu_star) to be any admissible constant at least 6 L_V, for instance C_rd(nu_star)=6(2 |V_1| + 3 |V_3| + 8 |V_4|), gives the stated inequality. The proof uses only the support-size premise on nu_star; prop:support-at-most-three supplies that premise when nu_star is chosen as a continuous optimizer.

added_lemmas.statement: Let nu = sum_{ell=1}^K w_ell delta_{u_ell} be in N(pbar), with K <= 3, M,m positive integers, N=Mm, and N pbar in Z. The grid-split rounding handle can be run to produce pi_rd in Pi_{M,m}(pbar) and a coupling gamma_rd of nu and L_M(pi_rd) such that integral |u-v| d gamma_rd(u,v) <= 2K/M + 2/m <= 6/M + 2/m.
added_lemmas.proof_tex: Choose integers n_1,...,n_K with sum_ell n_ell = M and |n_ell/M - w_ell| <= 1/M for every ell, for example by the usual largest-remainder rounding of the masses. Let nu_M^0 = sum_ell (n_ell/M) delta_{u_ell}. Since nu and nu_M^0 are supported on the same K points, couple the common mass at each point to itself and move only the excess mass. The total excess mass is at most sum_ell |n_ell/M - w_ell| <= K/M, and all moves occur inside [0,1], so the transport cost from nu to nu_M^0 is at most K/M. Now form an M-vector x by listing n_ell copies of u_ell for each ell. Its empirical law is nu_M^0. Replace each x_j by y_j = floor(m x_j)/m, with the convention y_j=1 when x_j=1. Then y_j belongs to {0,1/m,...,1}, |y_j-x_j| <= 1/m, and the paired empirical laws have average displacement at most 1/m. Let S = N pbar, an integer by hypothesis, and let T = sum_{j=1}^M m y_j. If S > T, increase grid coordinates one unit at a time, never above m; if S < T, decrease grid coordinates one unit at a time, never below 0. This is feasible because 0 <= S <= M m, so the total upward capacity is M m - T >= S - T when S >= T, and the total downward capacity is T >= T - S when S <= T. Let z_j be the resulting grid coordinates divided by m, and set pi_rd=(z_1,...,z_M). Then pi_rd is on the 1/m grid and M^{-1} sum_j pi_rd,j = S/(M m)=pbar, so pi_rd lies in Pi_{M,m}(pbar). This is exactly the mass-round, grid-split, and final-balancing procedure described in def:grid-split-rounding. The cost of the final balancing coupling from the empirical law of y to the empirical law of z is M^{-1} sum_j |z_j-y_j| = |S-T|/(M m). Since nu has mean pbar and |M^{-1} sum_j x_j - pbar| <= K/M while |M^{-1} sum_j y_j - M^{-1} sum_j x_j| <= 1/m, we have |S-T|/(M m) = |pbar - M^{-1} sum_j y_j| <= K/M + 1/m. Concatenating the three couplings gives a coupling of nu and L_M(pi_rd) with cost at most K/M + 1/m + K/M + 1/m = 2K/M + 2/m. Since K <= 3, this is at most 6/M + 2/m.

added_lemmas.statement: Let eta and nu be probability laws on [0,1], and let gamma be any coupling of eta and nu with integral |u-v| d gamma(u,v) <= Delta. Then, for the functional V built from centered moments around pbar in [0,1], |V(eta)-V(nu)| <= (2 |V_1| + 3 |V_3| + 8 |V_4|) Delta.
added_lemmas.proof_tex: For r=2,3,4 set g_r(u)=(u-pbar)^r. Because u and pbar lie in [0,1], |u-pbar| <= 1, and the derivative bound |g_r'(u)| = r |u-pbar|^{r-1} <= r gives |g_r(u)-g_r(v)| <= r |u-v| for all u,v in [0,1]. Integrating this inequality under gamma yields |m_r(eta)-m_r(nu)| <= r Delta for r=2,3,4. Also 0 <= m_2(eta),m_2(nu) <= 1, so |m_2(eta)^2 - m_2(nu)^2| <= |m_2(eta)-m_2(nu)| |m_2(eta)+m_2(nu)| <= 2 |m_2(eta)-m_2(nu)| <= 4 Delta. Using def:variance-functional and cancelling V_0, we get |V(eta)-V(nu)| <= |V_1| |m_2(eta)-m_2(nu)| + |V_3| |m_3(eta)-m_3(nu)| + |V_4| (|m_4(eta)-m_4(nu)| + |m_2(eta)^2-m_2(nu)^2|) <= (2 |V_1| + 3 |V_3| + 8 |V_4|) Delta.
